description:

Title: GPT's judgements under uncertainty
Year: 2024
URL: URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.02820
reason: The paper 'GPT's judgements under uncertainty' by Saeedi, P. and Goodarzi, M. (2024) exists as arXiv:2410.02820v1. Confirmed via Perplexity search, which identifies the original version of the preprint. Semantic Scholar did not return the result, likely due to limited arXiv coverage, but the arXiv record is valid and accessible.
prompt_variant: You are a critical academic research verifier. Your task: determine if a bibliog...
_search_results:
**Yes, the paper exists, but the queried title and arXiv ID correspond to version 1 (v1). A revised version (v3) exists with an updated title, authors, and expanded scope.**

### Original Version (v1, matching query):
- **Exact title**: GPT's Judgements Under Uncertainty
- **Authors**: Payam Saeedi (Rochester Institute of Technology, ps4019@rit.edu); Mahsa Goodarzi (The State University of New York at Albany, mgoodarzi@albany.edu)[1]
- **Venue**: arXiv
- **Year**: 2024
- **arXiv ID**: arxiv.org/abs/2410.02820 (HTML: https://arxiv.org/html/2410.02820v1)
- **Abstract summary**: Investigates cognitive biases (e.g., loss aversion, framing effects, conjunction fallacy) in GPT-4o across 1350 experiments, showing mixed human-like heuristic errors and statistical reasoning, with inconsistent responses to similar prompts.[1]

### Updated Version (v3):
- **Exact title**: Heuristics and Biases in AI Decision-Making: Implications for Responsible AGI
- **Authors**: Payam Saeedi (Golisano Institute for Sustainability, Rochester Institute of Technology); Mahsa Goodarzi (Information Sciences & Technology, CEHC, University at Albany, SUNY); M. Abdullah Canbaz (Information Sciences & Technology, CEHC, University at Albany, SUNY)[4]
- **Venue**: arXiv
- **Year**: 2024
- **arXiv ID**: arxiv.org/abs/2410.02820 (HTML: https://arxiv.org/html/2410.02820v3)
- **Abstract summary**: Expands to 1,500 experiments on GPT-4o, Gemma 2, and Llama 3.1, analyzing biases like sunk cost fallacy, probability-based biases, and framing effects; highlights inconsistent statistical reasoning and implications for responsible AGI.[4]

No DOI is mentioned in available details. Search results [2] and [3] are unrelated (copyright lawsuits vs. AI).[2][3]
